g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of interrupt stack design in the 8051 microcontroller. This is crucial for embedded systems.
g.text: Basic Interrupt Stack Design and Implementation

g.data-narration: Interrupts enable microcontrollers to respond immediately to time-critical events without constant polling of peripheral devices.
g.text: • Handle time-critical events instantly without polling
• Enable efficient multi-tasking in embedded systems
• Essential for timers, serial communication, and external triggers

g.data-narration: The 8051 has five interrupt sources: two external, two timer, and one serial port interrupt with fixed vector addresses.
The 8051 stack pointer (SP) is an 8-bit register that points to the next available stack location in internal RAM.
In 8051, the stack grows upward: SP is incremented first, then data is stored at the address pointed by SP.
Interrupt Service Routines in 8051 C use the 'interrupt' keyword followed by the vector number.
When an interrupt occurs, the 8051 automatically pushes the Program Counter onto the stack (2 bytes: PCH, PCL).
Beyond automatic PC saving, ISRs must save registers like A, PSW, DPTR if modified. The compiler handles most automatically.
The IE register controls interrupt enabling. EA (bit 7) is the global enable; individual bits enable specific interrupts.
The IP register sets interrupt priority (high/low). High priority interrupts can interrupt low priority ones, but not vice versa.
Nested interrupts require careful stack management. Each nesting level adds PC and context to the stack, increasing memory usage.
RETI is essential: it pops PC, clears the interrupt flag, and re-enables same-priority interrupts. Never use RET in ISRs.
g.text: Pops PC + clears flag + enables interrupts

g.data-narration: Always initialize the stack pointer in main() before enabling interrupts. SP = 0x7F maximizes stack space in standard 8051.
Key points: Five interrupt sources with fixed vectors, SP increments before push, stack grows upward in internal RAM.
g.text: ✓ Five interrupt sources: INT0, TF0, INT1, TF1, Serial (vectors: 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H)
✓ SP increments BEFORE push - stack grows upward in internal RAM (00H-7FH)
✓ ISR uses "interrupt N" keyword; RETI is mandatory (not RET)
✓ IE register: EA bit 7 global enable; IP sets priority levels
✓ Initialize SP to 0x7F or higher before enabling interrupts
✓ Use different register banks or save/restore context in ISRs

g.data-narration: Exam reminders: Memorize vector addresses, remember SP behavior, know IE/IP bit positions, and always use RETI in ISRs.
g.text: ❌ Common Mistakes: Forgetting SP init | Using RET instead of RETI | Not setting EA bit | Forgetting to save registers
✓ Quick Recall: Vector addresses (0003, 000B, 0013, 001B, 0023) | IE bits (EA,ES,ET1,EX1,ET0,EX0) | SP = 0x7F
